facts.content: Paramahansa Yogananda was an Indian guru who taught the path of Kriya Yoga.
Paramahansa Yogananda founded the Self-Realization Fellowship to teach Kriya Yoga, which he presented as a scientific technique for attaining a direct, personal experience of God.
The lineage of Kriya Yoga practice includes Mahavatar Babaji, Lahiri Mahasaya, Swami Sri Yukteswar, Paramahansa Yogananda, and Roy Eugene Davis.
Paramahansa Yogananda taught the path of Kriya Yoga, which he described as the 'jet-airplane' route to God, consisting of ancient yoga techniques to hasten the spiritual evolution of the student.

The Yogoda Satsanga Lessons are a home-study series containing thousands of pages of Paramahansa Yogananda's personal instructions on meditation techniques, including Kriya Yoga, and guidance on balanced spiritual living.
Paramahansa Yogananda founded the Self-Realization Fellowship (SRF) and the Yogoda Satsanga Society of India (YSS), which are religious meditation and Kriya Yoga organizations.
Kriya Yoga was the method of God-realization taught by Paramahansa Yogananda.
Paramhansa Yogananda initiated Mahatma Gandhi into Kriya Yoga, which is described as the highest technique on Yogananda’s path of Self-realization.
Paramhansa Yogananda stated that the Kriya Yoga technique is the fastest route to Self-realization.

The Kriya Yoga technique was hidden in secrecy for many centuries until 1861, when Mahavatar Babaji taught the technique to his disciple Lahiri Mahasaya. Lahiri Mahasaya then taught the technique to his disciple Sri Yukteswar, who taught it to his disciples, including Paramhansa Yogananda, who brought it to the West.
Paramahansa Yogananda was a practitioner of Hinduism and the Kriya Yoga philosophy.
The parents of Paramahansa Yogananda were disciples of Lahiri Mahasaya, a master who reintroduced Kriya Yoga in modern India.
Swami Sri Yukteswar Giri told Paramahansa Yogananda that he had been chosen to disseminate the ancient science of Kriya Yoga in America and worldwide.

Mahavatar Babaji visited Paramahansa Yogananda shortly before his departure for the West and stated: “You are the one I have chosen to spread the message of Kriya Yoga in the West. Long ago I met your guru Yukteswar at a Kumbha Mela; I told him then I would send you to him for training. Kriya Yoga, the scientific technique of God-realization, will ultimately spread in all lands, and aid in harmonizing the nations through man's personal, transcendental perception of the Infinite Father.”
Paramahansa Yogananda initiated more than 100,000 students into the techniques of Kriya Yoga during his thirty years of teaching in the West.
During his year-long sojourn in India starting in 1935, Paramahansa Yogananda gave classes and Kriya Yoga initiation in cities throughout the subcontinent.
During his 1935-1936 visit to India, Paramahansa Yogananda met with Mahatma Gandhi (who requested Kriya Yoga initiation), physicist Sir C. V. Raman, Ramana Maharshi, and Anandamoyi Ma.
Paramahansa Yogananda was an Indian yogi and guru who introduced many westerners to the teachings of meditation and Kriya Yoga through his book, Autobiography of a Yogi.

According to Philip Goldberg's account of Yogananda's autobiography, Mahavatar Babaji visited Paramahansa Yogananda in his room while he was in deep prayer and told him that he was chosen to spread Kriya Yoga to the West.

In his published work, The Self-Realization Fellowship Lessons, Paramahansa Yogananda provides in-depth instruction in the practice of the highest yoga science of God-realization, which is embodied in the specific principles and meditation techniques of Kriya Yoga.
Kriya Yoga serves as the foundation of Paramahansa Yogananda's teachings.
Kriya Yoga is defined as 'union (yoga) with the Infinite through a certain action or rite (kriya)', with the Sanskrit root 'kri' meaning to do, to act, and to react.

The Kriya Yoga spiritual lineage consists of Mahavatar Babaji, who taught the technique to Lahiri Mahasaya, who then taught it to his disciple Swami Sri Yukteswar Giri, who was the Guru of Paramahansa Yogananda.

Paramahansa Yogananda described the Kriya Yoga technique in his book, Autobiography of a Yogi: 'The Kriya Yogi mentally directs his life energy to revolve, upward and downward, around the six spinal centers (medullary, cervical, dorsal, lumbar, sacral, and coccygeal plexuses) which correspond to the twelve astral signs of the zodiac, the symbolic Cosmic Man. One-half-minute of revolution of energy around the sensitive spinal cord of man effects subtle progress in his evolution; that half-minute of Kriya equals one year of natural spiritual unfoldment.'

Mrinalini Mata, a former president of Self-Realization Fellowship/Yogoda Satsanga Society of India, stated: 'Kriya Yoga is so effective, so complete, because it brings God's love – the universal power through which God draws all souls back to reunion with Him – into operation in the devotee's life.'

Paramahansa Yogananda wrote in Autobiography of a Yogi that the actual Kriya Yoga technique should be learned from an authorized Kriyaban (Kriya Yogi) of the Self-Realization Fellowship or the Yogoda Satsanga Society of India.

Minott W. Lewis, a Boston dentist, was the first disciple to receive Kriya Yoga initiation from Paramahansa Yogananda in America; he subsequently helped establish the first Self-Realization Fellowship center and served as the organization's vice president for many years.
Paramhansa Yogananda emphasized the universality of the teachings of Sanaatan Dharma and the practice of Kriya Yoga as the religion of the dawning new age, known as Dwapara Yuga.
The Ananda Spiritual Travels website offers a free introduction to the path of Kriya Yoga.
Kriya Yoga is a sacred spiritual science that originated in India millenniums ago.

The Self-Realization Fellowship teaches methods of concentration and meditation, including a technique called kriya yoga, to help practitioners attain what Paramahansa Yogananda defined as Self-realization, or the realization of one's true Self or soul.
Self-Realization Fellowship teaches a technique called Kriya Yoga to prepared students, which is intended to hasten the process of spiritual awakening.

The practice of Kriya Yoga is intended to withdraw energy and attention from distracting thoughts, emotions, and stimuli, allowing the practitioner to experience peace and attunement with God in the resulting stillness.
To learn Kriya Yoga, Self-Realization Fellowship requires aspiring disciples to first complete a home-study course called the SRF Lessons, which are compiled from Yogananda's teachings.

In Self-Realization Fellowship, learning the Kriya Yoga technique is considered diksha (initiation) by the guru Yogananda, involving an initiation rite conducted by an authorized representative; disciples may receive printed lessons even if they cannot attend the rite.
Paramahansa Yogananda addressed a conference of religious liberals in Boston in 1920, having been sent by his guru, Babaji, to spread the message of kriya yoga to the West.

The Self Realization Fellowship teaches Kriya Yoga, a form of meditation, alongside five other branches: Asanas (yogic postures), Pranayama (breathing exercises), Mudras (hand gestures), Bandhas (muscle locks), and Dharana (concentration).
Paramahansa Yogananda founded Kriya Yoga and the Self-Realization Fellowship.
Paramahansa Yogananda introduced millions of Westerners to meditation and Kriya Yoga through his book, 'Autobiography of a Yogi'.
Kriya Yoga is defined as union with the infinite through a specific action or rite.
Kriya Yoga is an ancient science of the soul that offers methods for awakening higher spiritual consciousness and the inner bliss of divine realization.
Sri Yukteswar encouraged his disciples to practice Kriya Yoga as a way to navigate the 'darkness of maya'.

Babaji directed his disciple, Lahiri Mahasaya, to reintroduce the meditation science of Kriya Yoga to the world because people in the present scientific age were better prepared to receive higher knowledge.
Lahiri Mahasaya (1828-1895) was initiated by Babaji in 1861 and lived as a married householder in Varanasi, India, while teaching Kriya Yoga.

The book 'Two Souls: Four Lives: The Lives and Former Lives of Paramhansa Yogananda and His Disciple, Swami Kriyananda' explores the Kriya Yoga lineage transmitted by Paramhansa Yogananda and carried forward by Swami Kriyananda, emphasizing themes of reincarnation and the continuity of spiritual purpose across lifetimes.
Members of the Yogoda Satsanga Society of India who have taken the sacred initiation in Kriya Yoga are considered disciples of Paramahansa Yoganandaji.
Babaji told Paramhansa Yogananda, 'You are the one I have chosen to spread the message of Kriya Yoga in the West.'

“He touched my forehead. Masses of whirling light appeared; the radiance gradually formed itself into the opal-blue spiritual eye, ringed in gold and centered with a white pentagonal star. “ ’Penetrate your consciousness through the star into the kingdom of the Infinite.’ “My guru’s voice had a new note, soft like distant music. Vision after vision broke as oceanic surf on the shores of my soul. The panoramic spheres finally melted in a sea of bliss. I lost myself in ever-surging blessedness. When I returned hours later to awareness of this world, the master gave me the technique of Kriya Yoga.”
Paramhansa Yogananda described his mission as a 'spiritual invasion of America' intended to introduce Kriya Yoga and yogic teachings to Western society to counterbalance materialism.
Paramhansa Yogananda founded the Self-Realization Fellowship in 1920 to disseminate Kriya Yoga globally.

The book 'Two Souls: Four Lives' draws parallels between the leadership of William the Conqueror and Paramhansa Yogananda, noting that both consolidated fragmented entities—William through Norman rule in England and Yogananda through disseminating Kriya Yoga and establishing spiritual communities worldwide.

Within modern yoga and Kriya Yoga communities, 'Two Souls: Four Lives' contributes to discussions on reincarnation by offering historical parallels that illustrate how advanced souls may influence key turning points in human development.

Readers within yoga and Kriya Yoga circles often describe 'Two Souls: Four Lives' as convincing and inspirational, noting its value in strengthening belief in karmic patterns and the enduring impact of spiritual figures across lifetimes.

Swami Yukterwar Giri believed Paramahansa Yogananda's purpose was to spread kriya yoga and meditation to the West, specifically to lead people to God by expounding the truth of original yoga and original Christianity, and to show the underlying unity of all religions.
Paramahansa Yogananda traveled to America with the mission of spreading the Kriya Yoga path worldwide.
Sri Yukteswar taught that the darkness of maya is approaching and reminded his disciples of their need for Kriya Yoga.
The book 'Autobiography of a Yogi' is a non-fiction memoir that recounts the life of Paramahansa Yogananda, his search for his guru, and his teachings on Kriya Yoga.
Reading Autobiography of a Yogi is an unofficial prerequisite for prospective students of the Self-Realization Fellowship's "Lessons for Home Study" before initiation into Kriya Yoga.
Paramahansa Yogananda (1893–1952) introduced Kriya Yoga meditation to the West through his organization, the Self-Realization Fellowship (SRF).
Dr. M. W. Lewis was the first American to receive Kriya Yoga initiation in 1920 and served as a longtime vice-president of the Self-Realization Fellowship.

A direct disciple of Paramahansa Yogananda is defined as an individual who personally encountered Yogananda during his lifetime (1893–1952), received direct spiritual guidance or initiation (such as into Kriya Yoga) from him, and actively participated in the propagation of his teachings on self-realization.

Direct disciples of Paramahansa Yogananda are distinguished from later adherents who were initiated into Kriya Yoga by Self-Realization Fellowship-authorized ministers after 1952, or from indirect followers trained under figures like Daya Mata.
Paramahansa Yogananda met his master, Swami Sri Yukteswar Giri, in 1910 in Benares (Varanasi), India, and became his disciple, undergoing training in Kriya Yoga.
Paramahansa Yogananda founded the Yogoda Satsanga Society (YSS) of India in 1917 to disseminate Kriya Yoga.
Paramahansa Yogananda initiated thousands of students into Kriya Yoga techniques through personal instruction and structured lessons.

Paramahansa Yogananda's line of gurus stated: "Kriya Yoga, the scientific technique of God-realization, will ultimately spread in all lands, and aid in harmonizing the nations through man’s personal, transcendental perception of the Infinite Father."
The first disciple to take Kriya Yoga initiation in America was a student of Paramahansa Yogananda.
All members of Self-Realization Fellowship, including monastics and lay members, who have taken the sacred initiation in Kriya Yoga are considered disciples of Paramahansa Yogananda.
The Kriya Yoga technique was taught by Bhagavan Krishna to Arjuna and is referenced in the Bhagavad Gita chapters IV:29 and V:27–28.
Mahavatar Babaji revived the Kriya Yoga technique for modern man, which is currently taught by the Gurus of Self-Realization Fellowship and Yogoda Satsanga Society of India.
Mahavatar Babaji revived the lost scientific meditation technique of Kriya Yoga in the current age.

Mahavatar Babaji stated to his disciple Lahiri Mahasaya: “The Kriya Yoga that I am giving to the world through you in this nineteenth century is a revival of the same science that Krishna gave millenniums ago to Arjuna; and that was later known to Patanjali and Christ, and to St. John, St. Paul, and other disciples.”

Mahavatar Babaji appeared to Paramahansa Yogananda at his father's home in Kolkata shortly before Yogananda left for America in 1920 and instructed him to spread the message of Kriya Yoga in the West.

Lahiri Mahasaya is recognized as a seminal figure in the renaissance of yoga that began in modern India in the latter part of the nineteenth century because he was the first to teach the lost ancient Kriya science in contemporary times.

Paramahansa Yogananda stated that Self-Realization Fellowship teaches the universal truth underlying both the original Christianity of Jesus Christ and the original Yoga of Krishna, using the science of Kriya Yoga sent by Mahavatar Babaji.

The inauguration of the Kriya Yoga movement in modern times included a milestone in the understanding of the guru-disciple relationship as practiced in Self-Realization Fellowship and Yogoda Satsanga Society.

Paramahansa Yogananda explained that for ages, Kriya Yoga was hidden from the general public to ensure the ancient holy science was known only to qualified chelas, such as monks and other renunciants, whose lives were dedicated to God-realization.
Mahavatar Babaji waived the ancient restrictions of asceticism and renunciation regarding Kriya Yoga so that the masses might benefit from the practice.

Mahavatar Babaji required Lahiri Mahasaya and all descendants of his spiritual line, specifically the Self-Realization Fellowship (SRF) and Yogoda Satsanga Society (YSS) line of Gurus, to impose a period of preliminary spiritual training on anyone seeking Kriya Yoga initiation.
In 1920, Mahavatar Babaji visited Paramahansa Yogananda and blessed him to journey to America to spread the teachings of Kriya Yoga worldwide.
During his early years in America, Paramahansa Yogananda personally approved every individual who received Kriya Yoga initiation.

In 1951–52, Paramahansa Yogananda established rules for bestowing Kriya Yoga initiation, requiring that it be bestowed only on those who became members of Self-Realization Fellowship (SRF) or Yogoda Satsanga Society (YSS) and formally accepted the guru-disciple relationship.

Paramahansa Yogananda referred to those who received Kriya Yoga initiation through his instrumentality as “disciples of the order,” considering all SRF/YSS devotees who took the Kriya Pledge as belonging to an order of discipleship, regardless of whether they took vows in the monastic order.

The Self-Realization Church of All Religions was renamed The Self-Revelation Church of Absolute Monism and currently holds services based on the teachings of Paramahansa Yogananda with a focus on Kriya Yoga meditation.
Paramahansa Yogananda was blessed by Mahavatar Babaji, Lahiri Mahasaya, and Swami Sri Yukteswar to disseminate Kriya Yoga worldwide.

Bob Raymer, a former commercial airline pilot, met Paramahansa Yogananda in the latter years of Yogananda's life, was initiated into Kriya Yoga, and was made a minister of the Self-Realization Fellowship.
Swami Premananda established the Self-Revelation Church of Absolute Monism to propagate Kriya Yoga and non-dual philosophy in line with Paramhansa Yogananda's teachings.

The disciples of Paramahansa Yogananda from the 1920s established the foundational infrastructure of the Self-Realization Fellowship, including regional centers and administrative and editorial efforts, which facilitated the expansion of Kriya Yoga teachings.
Disciples who joined Self-Realization Fellowship in the 1940s contributed to the organization's growth and later pursued diverse paths in disseminating Kriya Yoga and spiritual principles.

The Sunburst Brotherhood, founded by Norman Paulsen, established a community in California that emphasized eco-spirituality, sustainable living, and the integration of Kriya Yoga practices with environmental stewardship.
Bob Raymer received Kriya Yoga initiation at the Hollywood Temple one day after meeting Paramahansa Yogananda.

Brother Mokshananda served as a minister for the Self-Realization Fellowship (SRF) in the years following 1952, contributing to the continuity of Yogananda's Kriya Yoga teachings through monastic life and public outreach.

The teachings of Paramahansa Yogananda are disseminated through the printed Self-Realization Fellowship Lessons, a comprehensive home-study series that provides instruction in Kriya Yoga meditation techniques and balanced spiritual living.

Self-Realization Fellowship engages in several activities, including: publishing the writings, lectures, and recorded talks of Paramahansa Yogananda and his monastic disciples; operating retreats, temples, and meditation centers; publishing a quarterly magazine titled 'Self-Realization'; providing spiritual training for monks and nuns; conducting lecture tours and classes on Kriya Yoga, including an annual weeklong World Convocation; offering programs on meditation and spiritual living for children and teens; offering individual spiritual guidance; supporting charitable relief and welfare activities; and guiding the Worldwide Prayer Circle.

Paramahansa Yogananda named the Mount Washington location the SRF Mother Center and established it as a training center for Self-Realization Fellowship monastics and an administrative center for disseminating the science of Kriya Yoga worldwide.

Paramahansa Yogananda established the International Headquarters of Self-Realization Fellowship as a training center for SRF monastics and an administrative center for disseminating the science of Kriya Yoga worldwide.
The Self-Realization Fellowship was founded to disseminate Paramahansa Yogananda's teachings on Kriya Yoga.
Paramahansa Yogananda introduced a form of yoga called kriya yoga, which focuses on life energy and breath control, to the United States in the 1920s.
Paramahansa Yogananda, a Hindu spiritual leader, spoke about Kriya Yoga in Boston in 1920.
Isha Kriya is a guided kriya yoga meditation that focuses on seated meditation and an internal action, which is a specific thought.

A breath-based kriya yoga intervention, utilizing breath practices similar to Shambhavi Mahamudra, resulted in a significant reduction in anxiety and depression symptoms in patients diagnosed with mood and anxiety disorders.
Kriya yoga interventions using breath-based techniques were reported to reduce the perception of stress and pain in breast cancer patients compared to control groups.
Studies using breath-based kriya yoga techniques reported improved stress regulation, sympathovagal balance, and workload tolerance in practitioners compared to control groups.
Kriya Yoga practice results in relaxing effects on the brain, as evidenced by EEG absolute power analysis, Perceived Stress Scale (PSS) scores, and participants' subjective responses.

Kriya Yoga is a practice that functions as a science-supported aid for mental clarity and emotional balance, originating from traditions dating back centuries and popularized globally by Paramahansa Yogananda.

Kriya Yoga was brought to the West by Paramahansa Yogananda and reintroduced to the modern world by Lahiri Mahasaya, gaining significant recognition following the release of the book 'Autobiography of a Yogi'.
The practice of Kriya Yoga consists of deep meditation, mantra repetition, a series of controlled breathing exercises (pranayama), and rigorous ethical living.
Kriya Yoga aims to bring the practitioner into a state of superconscious awareness and calm mental turbulence.
Practitioners of Kriya Yoga report experiencing better memory, emotional balance, reduced stress, and enhanced mental health.
Controlled breathing exercises in Kriya Yoga influence the nervous system by helping to maintain heart rate and cortisol levels, which aids in calming the body and improving mind-body coherence.
Regulated breathing, as utilized in Kriya Yoga, stimulates the vagus nerve, which regulates mood, stress tolerance, and immunity.
Kriya Yoga practice enhances neuroplasticity, defined as the brain's ability to adapt and transform through the creation of new neural pathways.
Kriya Yoga practice increases the supply of blood and oxygen to the prefrontal cortex, the brain region governing concentration, judgment, and impulse control.
The rhythmic, deep breathing methods of Kriya Yoga regulate the amygdala, stabilizing it and reducing susceptibility to emotional outbursts during stressful situations.
Regular Kriya Yoga practice improves sleep quality, which augments the brain's nightly 'clean-up' process, thereby improving cognitive function and minimizing the risk of neurodegenerative disease.

Kriya Yoga offers therapeutic value for managing depression, anxiety, emotional pain, and trauma disorders like PTSD by facilitating emotional regulation through deliberate breathwork and inner focus.
Kriya Yoga develops psychological resilience by teaching practitioners to remain grounded, emotionally balanced, and directed, rather than reacting to life's challenges with fear or avoidance.
Kriya Yoga integrates with modern psychological concepts such as mindfulness-based cognitive therapy, utilizing time-to-time awareness as a tool for managing emotions and self-consciousness.
Numerous modern psychologists, therapists, and neuroscientists are studying the benefits of Kriya Yoga and recommending it as a therapeutic model for long-term mental health care.
Ancient breath-based spiritual practices like Kriya Yoga are supported by biochemical research, EEG readings, and fMRI scans.
Kriya Yoga serves as a link between self-transformation and scientific legitimacy due to its combination of introspective wisdom and empirical inquiry.

The process for learning Kriya Yoga involves: (1) learning the technique from a certified instructor or a recognized spiritual lineage, (2) introducing meditation and basic breath awareness into a daily routine, and (3) practicing consistently, starting with 10 to 15 minutes per day.
Kriya Yoga addresses the fundamental causes of mental distress, whereas medication primarily works on symptoms.
Kriya Yoga provides benefits including augmented concentration, stress alleviation, emotional resilience, and mental acumen.
Kriya Yoga functions as a psychological instrument that conditions the mind toward peace and purpose.
Kriya Yoga serves as a link to long-term mental health as contemporary psychology incorporates traditional knowledge.

Systematic practice of Kriya Yoga leads to significant improvements in attention, decision-making, and work efficiency, while mitigating burnout and fostering resilience.

The review of Kriya Yoga integrates insights from classical yogic texts including the Bhagavad Gita, Yoga Sutras of Patanjali, Mandukya Upanishad, and Hatha Yoga Pradipika.

Kriya yoga significantly reduces perceived stress and anxiety among healthcare workers, according to the study 'Kriya yoga, an easily deliverable tool for stress management among health care workers: Presenting electroencephalographic evidences'.
Electroencephalographic (EEG) analysis of healthcare workers after 6 weeks of practicing Kriya yoga shows increased alpha power and decreased stress levels.
Participants in the study 'Kriya yoga, an easily deliverable tool for stress management among health care workers' reported enhanced feelings of calmness and well-being following Kriya yoga sessions.

The study 'Kriya yoga, an easily deliverable tool for stress management among health care workers' suggests implementing Kriya yoga as a tool for mental health support among healthcare workers during crises.

The study on Kriya yoga found that 6 weeks of practice significantly increased alpha power in the frontal, central, and parietal regions of the brain, and increased delta power in the central and parietal areas of healthcare workers.

Healthcare workers participating in the Kriya yoga intervention showed marked improvements in Perceived Stress Scale (PSS) scores and reduced levels of anxiety, depression, and stress as measured by DASS-21 scales.
The Kriya yoga intervention for stress management consisted of a six-week program including breath awareness, Anulom Vilom, Om chanting, Gayatri Mantra, and Shavasan.

The Kriya yoga study utilized a randomized design where participants were assigned to intervention and control groups, with EEG and stress measures collected at multiple time points throughout the study.
Participants in the Kriya yoga intervention reported subjective feelings of calmness, well-being, and ease of practice as notable outcomes.

The Kriya Yoga study protocol involves a 25-minute cycle, followed by a yoga-nidra session, eight minutes of silent relaxation, and twelve minutes of meditation instructions focused on body awareness, starting at the feet and progressing to the head.

Theta peak amplitude increases significantly during the Kriya yoga period and remains elevated in the frontal, temporal, and parietal regions (FZ, F8, P4, PZ, T4, T6), with statistics for the PZ channel showing F(4,164) = 13.24, p < 0.00001.

Statistical differences in theta peak amplitude were observed between the Kriya yoga period (M = 0.13, SD = 0.11) and the pre-resting state (M = 0.09, SD = 0.07), as well as between the Yoga-nidra period (M = 0.15, SD = 0.06) and the pranayama period (M = 0.08, SD = 0.07).

Tukey’s post hoc analysis of theta band-width showed significant differences between the Yoga-nidra period (M = 0.63, SD = 0.25) and the three earlier phases: pre-resting state (M = 0.25, SD = 0.24), pranayama (M = 0.20, SD = 0.17), and Kriya yoga (M = 0.38, SD = 0.25).

Theta peak frequency remains consistent across right frontotemporal and parieto-occipital regions (T4, T5, T6, P3, P4, PZ, O1, OZ) until the Kriya yoga period, after which it decreases during the post-resting phase (PZ Channel: F(4,164) = 6.96, p < 0.00001).

Kriya Yoga teachings assert that cosmic life-energy (prana) enters the body at the medulla oblongata, which acts as the "main switch that controls the entrance, storage, and distribution of the life-force."
Kriya Yoga teachings identify the medulla oblongata as the twin pole of the ajna chakra (also known as the agya chakra, Christ center, or spiritual eye).

In Kriya Yoga, prana moves from the medulla oblongata upwards into the higher brain and downwards to various bodily chakras, with significant storage in the sahasrara at the top of the brain in a yogi.
Kriya Yoga posits that ego-consciousness is centered in the medulla.
Ramana Maharshi would object to the Kriya Yoga interpretation of the "sun" and "moon" as derived from the Bhagavad-Gita.
Kriya Yoga teaches that when attention is withdrawn from the physical body, it enters three progressively deeper astral nadis: the vajra, the chitra, and the brahmanadi.
Kriya Yoga teaches that energy and consciousness pass through the chitra nadi into the brahmanadi, which is the spine of the causal body.
Kriya Yoga teaches that Brahma, the Creative aspect of AUM, descended into outward manifestation through the brahmanadi.
Kriya Yoga teaches that the soul must ascend through the brahmanadi to reunite with the Spirit.
Kriya Yoga teaches that the opening of the brahmanadi is at the top of the head, and reaching this point allows the yogi to reunite with omnipresence.

Kriya Yoga teachings utilize Hatha Yoga methods (asana and pranayama) and Raja Yoga components (pratyahara, dharana, dhyana, and samadhi) with the goal of achieving Nirvikalpa Samadhi through ascended absorption at the ajna center and passage through the central brightness within the spiritual eye.

Kriya Yoga practice involves concentrating attention on lights and sounds in the brain core, which is similar to the practice of Shabd Yoga, though Shabd Yoga practitioners claim their method reaches higher states with less arduous sadhana.

Kriya Yoga makes claims regarding bodily transformation and rejuvenation that other forms of yoga do not, which can sometimes lead to an over-emphasis on these physical effects rather than the goal of realization.

Kriya Yoga is described as a psychophysiological method that decarbonizes human blood and recharges it with oxygen, which is then transmuted into life current to rejuvenate the brain and spinal centers.
Practitioners of Kriya Yoga claim that by stopping the accumulation of venous blood, the yogi can lessen or prevent the decay of tissues, and that an advanced yogi can transmute cells into energy.

In Kriya Yoga, the practitioner mentally directs life energy to revolve around six spinal centers (medullary, cervical, dorsal, lumbar, sacral, and coccygeal plexuses), which are said to correspond to the 12 astral signs of the zodiac.

Kriya Yoga employs asana and pranayama techniques to move subtle life energy up and down the spine, aiming to purify karmas associated with the six spinal chakras, with the goal of collecting life force at the ajna chakra (third eye) and proceeding to the sahasrar, which is considered the doorway to the infinite.

In Kriya Yoga, when life force collects at the ajna chakra, practitioners experience a visual phenomenon of a golden ring around a blue sky with a brilliant white star in the center; concentration on this star leads to passage beyond the 'three coils' and into Nirvikalpa Samadhi.
The Kriya Yoga tradition assumes that repeated immersion in Nirvikalpa Samadhi will naturally lead to the state of 'sahaj', which is the realization of that state in daily life.

The author of 'Paramahansa Yogananda and Kriya Yoga: A Comparative Analysis' posits that the Kriya Yoga realization appears to align with the Indian version of samadhi, where the practitioner attempts to hold onto the state of Nirvikalpa Samadhi for as long as possible, rather than the Ch'an version of sahaja.

The author of 'Paramahansa Yogananda and Kriya Yoga: A Comparative Analysis' argues that hyper-emotional bliss is distinct from the serene experience of reality, and that mystics must comprehend this distinction to progress beyond the stage of emotional joy.
Kriya Yoga posits that matter is crystallized or condensed Consciousness, whereas philosophic paths like Advaita Vedanta and various forms of Buddhism view all experiences as ideas in the Mind.

Shabd Yoga practitioners believe that separating from the physical, astral, and causal bodies leads the soul to a "super-causal" dimension, whereas Kriya Yoga practitioners believe this separation leads the soul directly to the Purushottama or nirvikalpa.

Kriya Yoga differs from Shabd Yoga in that Shabd Yoga teaches aspirants to bypass the path of prana (motor) currents and instead concentrate sensory currents at the third eye, while Kriya Yoga aims to bring both energy and consciousness up to the top of the spine.

Kriya Yoga describes the path after piercing the 'big star' as a passage beyond the three worlds, whereas Shabd Yoga teaches a hierarchy of seven planes beyond, with the Master or Master-Power serving as the chief aid in transcending from one plane to the next.

The seven stages or planes of creation mentioned in Kriya Yoga (Bhuloka, Bhuvarloka, Swarloka, Maharloka, Janaloka, Tapoloka, and Satyaloka) correspond to the traditionally delineated yoga stages listed in Sant Kirpal Singh's 'The Crown of Life: A Study in Yoga' (bhur, bhuva, swah, maha, janah, tapah and satyam).
Kriya Yoga teachings consider pranayama and kriya techniques essential preparation to attain the state of pratyahara, whereas Shabd Masters argue that these techniques are a waste of time.
The Kriya Yoga path assumes that more preparation is required to become capable of concentrating at the spiritual eye compared to the requirements of Shabd Yoga.

The author of 'Paramahansa Yogananda and Kriya Yoga: A Comparative Analysis' argues that Kriya Yoga's goals are closer to the Sant Mat view than to the ajata doctrine of Ramana Maharshi or the 'nothing ever happened' viewpoint of Papaji, and that Kriya Yoga ultimately aims for a form of heaven.

Kriya Yoga practitioners listen to sounds associated with the chakras progressively, starting from the muladhara chakra (root chakra) and moving upward. The sounds are: root chakra (hissing), 2nd chakra (crickets), 3rd chakra (pan flute or deep-throated whistling), 4th chakra (tinkling bells or heavy gong), 5th chakra (high-pitched whistling), 6th chakra (glorious trumpets), and the crown chakra (thunder followed by the AUM).

In Surat Shabd Yoga, the 'big bell' sound is heard from overhead and pulls the soul upward above the lower centers into the astral world, whereas in Kriya Yoga, the 'big bell' is sometimes listed as heard at the heart chakra.

Kriya Yoga posits that the pineal gland (third eye center) is linked with the medulla oblongata, and that the collection of pranas between these two points is necessary for 'lift-off' to the sahasrar (crown chakra).
Kriya Yoga focuses on the mechanics of energy in the body and cleansing karmas in the 'pranic centers' (bodily chakras), but places less emphasis on jnana (knowledge/wisdom) compared to other paths.

The study analyzed EEG data from 43 subjects who performed the SKY technique, divided into five phases: resting-pre, pranayama, kriya, yoga-nidra, and resting-post.

During the SKY meditation study, the yoga-nidra phase (M = 0.36, SD = 0.15) and the post-resting period (M = 0.23, SD = 0.19) exhibited lower alpha amplitude compared to the pre-resting state (M = 0.72, SD = 0.36), pranayama (M = 0.68, SD = 0.42), and kriya (M = 0.64, SD = 0.27) phases.

Delta amplitude is higher for the yoga-nidra period (M = 0.22, SD = 0.09) compared to the pre (M = 0.08, SD = 0.05), pranayama (M = 0.05, SD = 0.04), kriya (M = 0.12, SD = 0.08), and post (M = 0.11, SD = 0.10) phases, according to Tukey’s post hoc analysis.

Delta bandwidth in the T5 channel is significantly higher during the yoga-nidra phase (M = 0.74, SD = 0.29) compared to the pre (M = 0.23, SD = 0.18), pranayama (M = 0.13, SD = 0.12), and kriya (M = 0.33, SD = 0.20) phases.

Peak delta frequency in the T3 channel increases during the yoga-nidra period (M = 2.40, SD = 0.16) compared to the pre (M = 2.17, SD = 0.41), pranayama (M = 2.15, SD = 0.30), and kriya (M = 2.24, SD = 0.25) phases, before decreasing in the post phase (M = 2.32, SD = 0.34).

The aperiodic exponent in the FZ channel decreases significantly during the yoga-nidra (M = 0.68, SD = 0.17) and post-resting (M = 0.41, SD = 0.19) phases compared to the pre (M = 0.91, SD = 0.29), pranayama (M = 0.81, SD = 0.40), and kriya (M = 0.88, SD = 0.23) phases.

The kriya period of SKY meditation is marked by an increase in theta power, while the yoga-nidra period shows a decrease in alpha power, a sustained increase in theta and delta power, and a reduction in the 1/f aperiodic signal exponent.
